content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh

## Summary

Reviewed the lifecycle/backend-cleanup changes against `origin/master..HEAD`, including `src/cli/run.rs`, `src/contexts/project_run_record/queries.rs`, `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs`, and related adapter/test code. I found two correctness issues: forced stop still leaves orphaned backend groups running after the leader exits, and stale cleanup now hard-fails on dead legacy backend records without authoritative identity. I also ran `cargo test run_stop_refuses_unsafe_orphaned_backend_process_group_cleanup_after_leader_exit -- --nocapture`, which passes and confirms that the orphan-leak behavior is currently locked in.

## Amendments

1. `src/cli/run.rs:2629-2650` still refuses to kill an orphaned backend process group after its leader exits. `kill_stale_backend_process_group()` enumerates surviving members, but if any remain it returns an error instead of signaling `-pgid`, and that refusal bubbles through cleanup into `run stop`/stale recovery (`src/cli/run.rs:2700-2724`, `src/cli/run.rs:2811-2813`, `src/cli/run.rs:3475-3483`). The new regression test at `tests/cli.rs:6580-6684` even asserts that the orphan stays alive. This leaves the forced-stop case from the bead unresolved: a TERM-ignoring orchestrator can be SIGKILLed while its backend orphan keeps running. The stop/recovery path still needs a safe way to target the recorded backend group, rather than codifying the leak.
   Rationale: Forced stop is supposed to tear down backend subprocess trees. In the current implementation the orchestrator can die while backend work continues in the background.
2. `src/cli/run.rs:2632-2637` now rejects every tracked backend record whose `proc_start_*` fields are missing before it even checks whether that process is already gone. Because `RunBackendProcessRecord` deserializes those identity fields as optional (`src/adapters/fs.rs:177-187`) and `backend_process_is_authoritative()` only tests for their presence (`src/adapters/fs.rs:673-677`), a legacy or partially written `active-processes.json` entry with a dead PID now makes stale reconciliation and stop cleanup fail (`src/cli/run.rs:2811-2813`, `src/cli/run.rs:3475-3483`) even though there is nothing left to kill. The cleanup path should prune dead non-authoritative records and only refuse when such a record is still alive.
   Rationale: This is a backward-compatibility and robustness regression: older workspaces can get wedged on stale backend metadata that previous code would safely ignore.
